unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 4! In this unit you will explore the places in your community and the people who help you there. You will learn to name places like the hospital, the market, the court, the college and the garden, and to talk about helpers like the doctor, the officer and the sailor. You will describe places using words like quiet and busy, give clear instructions, ask questions with do and does, and explain your ideas with the word “because”. By the end, you will be ready to write your own report about a place in your community — just like Amal does in this unit's story.
unit.learningPath:
Start with Vocabulary and learn a few community words each day.
Work through the six Grammar lessons, from imperatives to quantifiers.
Read the story “From Our Village to the County” and the four shorter passages in Reading and story.
Answer the Comprehension questions, then move through Speaking, Writing and Activities.
Finish with the Quiz, My progress and the Self-Assessment statements.

visual.alt: Children exploring places and services in their community

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who helps sick people feel better.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The doctor works at the hospital.
practiceSentences: Doctor Sarah checks my temperature whenever I feel unwell.
A doctor asks careful questions to find the problem.
Does the doctor visit the school every term?
Theo said, "The doctor wrapped my sore ankle gently."
My uncle is a doctor in the village clinic.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - o - c - t - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your adult helper nearby, ask me to describe what Doctor Sarah does in one day, then use 'doctor' in your own sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who helps keep people safe and makes sure rules are followed.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The officer helped us cross the road.
practiceSentences: An officer stands at the crossing to slow the traffic.
An officer keeps the streets safe for everyone.
Where does the traffic officer stand each morning?
Nora said, "The officer helped the lost little boy."
I waved to the officer near the market.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: o - f - f - i - c - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with an adult and ask me three safety questions an officer might answer at a busy crossing.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who works on a boat or ship at sea.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sailor sails across the sea.
practiceSentences: A sailor learns to read the wind and the stars.
A sailor checks the ropes before the ship leaves.
Has the sailor travelled to faraway lands?
Sami said, "The sailor showed us his compass."
My great-grandfather was a sailor long ago.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - a - i - l - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to describe a sailor's job on a ship, then tell me two things a sailor needs at sea. Keep your adult helper beside you.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A place where doctors and nurses care for people who are sick or hurt.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My sister is resting at the hospital.
practiceSentences: Our county hospital opens its doors at every hour.
The hospital has a bright ward just for children.
Which bus goes past the hospital?
Adam said, "I got my cast at the hospital."
My mum took flowers to a friend in hospital.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - o - s - p - i - t - a - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult listening, ask me who you would meet inside a hospital, then name three of them back to me.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A place where people solve problems fairly using the laws.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The court is next to the police station.
practiceSentences: People go to the court when a problem needs solving.
The court settles problems fairly for the whole town.
Is the court near the police station?
Dad said, "A judge works inside the court."
I saw the tall court building on our walk.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to explain, in simple words, why a court helps people solve problems fairly. Have an adult with you.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A school for older students and adults.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My big brother goes to college in the city.
practiceSentences: Adam studies health and safety at the county college.
The college gates open early for morning classes.
What does your sister study at college?
Maya said, "My brother lives near his college."
I want to go to college like my aunt.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - l - l - e - g - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult nearby, ask me how a college is different from your school, then say the difference in one sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A place where people buy and sell food and other things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We bought bananas at the market.
practiceSentences: Omar sells ripe mangoes at his busy market stall.
The market is busiest early on Saturday mornings.
Can we buy sweet mangoes at the market?
Theo said, "The market smells of ripe oranges."
I help Mum carry vegetables from the market.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - a - r - k - e - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to walk you around a busy market, then describe two stalls using the word 'market'. Stay with your adult helper.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A place where plants, flowers or vegetables grow.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There are tomatoes in our school garden.
practiceSentences: Our class grows beans and carrots in the school garden.
Bees buzz around the flowers in the school garden.
Who waters the garden during the holidays?
Mina said, "Our garden grew the biggest pumpkin."
I planted mint in our garden with Grandma.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: g - a - r - d - e - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, ask me what grows in a school garden, then plan three plants for your own garden.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A door or way to go out of a place; to go out.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We used the back exit after the class.
practiceSentences: Every classroom shows a green sign above the exit.
The green sign shows the nearest exit.
Which exit leads to the playground?
The teacher said, "Walk calmly to the exit."
I found the exit quickly during the fire drill.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me where you would find an exit sign in a big building, and practise saying 'The exit is over there.' with an adult nearby.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small group of homes in the countryside, smaller than a town.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My grandmother lives in a quiet village.
practiceSentences: Amal walks past green farms on her way through the village.
The village has one school and a small library.
How far is the village from the city?
Sami said, "Everyone knows everyone in our village."
I love the quiet nights in my grandmother's village.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: v - i - l - l - a - g - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your adult helper, ask me to compare a quiet village with a busy town, then say which you would choose and why.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A part of a country that has its own towns and rules.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We live in this county near the hills.
practiceSentences: Every county in our country has its own busy towns.
Our county has hills, farms, and two busy towns.
Which county do your cousins live in?
Adam said, "Our county fair has a camel show."
I drew a map of our county for homework.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - u - n - t - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to put village, town, county and country in order from small to big. Check the order with an adult.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A line where two countries or places meet.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We crossed the border into a new country.
practiceSentences: A tall row of trees marks the border between two farms.
A river marks the border between the two countries.
Did you show your papers at the border?
Dad said, "The border town has a famous market."
I found the border drawn in red on the map.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - o - r - d - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask me what you might see at a border between two counties, then draw one on a map.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The name of the place where someone lives.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I wrote my home address on the letter.
practiceSentences: Grandma Hana keeps our address written inside her notebook.
Every letter needs a clear address on the front.
Can you write your address without any help?
Maya said, "My address has the house number first."
I memorised my address in case I get lost.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - d - d - r - e - s - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to check that you can say your full address clearly. Practise it only with your adult helper listening.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name places in your community and the people who work there.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Every place and helper word named correctly in Activity 1 and used again in the Speaking 1 recording.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Describe a place using adjectives and opposites, like quiet and busy.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four places labelled quiet or busy in Activity 4, each with a reason the teacher can follow.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Give simple instructions using imperatives (“Exit the court quietly.”).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four instructions written in Grammar 1 that each open with a base verb and leave out “you”.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk about routines using the simple present (“The doctor works at the hospital.”).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five verbs given the correct ending in Grammar 2, and five true present-tense facts in Writing 5.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Ask questions with do and does (“Where does the college open?”).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four correctly formed questions in Grammar 3, plus questions asked and answered in Speaking 4.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say what you can see, using can see (“I can see the market.”).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four completed can see sentences in Grammar 4, each naming a real object from the unit texts.
One because sentence in the Writing 3 report and one spoken reason in the Speaking 1 recording.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use some and a lot of to talk about amounts.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four quantifier blanks filled correctly in Grammar 6, and both phrases reused in the Writing 4 paragraph.

readings.title: Our Community
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: A community is a place where people live and help one another. In my county there are many places. At the hospital, a doctor helps people who are sick. At the market, people buy and sell food. At the college, older students learn new jobs. An officer keeps us safe on the road. We all live together, and we all help one another.

readings.title: A Poem: “Places I Know”
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
In my village the days are slow,
Past the garden the goats all go.
The market is busy, the court is grand,
So many places across our land!

readings.title: At the Market
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “On Friday we go to the market. It is busy! We buy bananas and rice. I can see a lot of people. I like the market because it is full of colours.”

readings.title: Community Helpers Song
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Sing along:
“The doctor helps you when you're sick, the officer keeps you safe. The sailor sails across the sea — our helpers make one place!”

readings.title: From Our Village to the County
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Amal lived in a small village near Mount Meru in northern Tanzania. The village was quiet, with tall trees and green farms. Goats walked slowly along the road.
Every Friday, Amal went with her class to learn about the community. One morning, Teacher Yasmin said, “Today we will visit the county centre.” The whole class clapped. Amal had never been there before.
They climbed into the big school bus, and Nadia the driver waved them aboard. “Look out of the window,” said Teacher Yasmin. “What can you see?”
“I can see the market,” said Sami. “There are a lot of people!”
“I can see a garden,” said Nora. “It has flowers and trees.”
Their first stop was the hospital. A kind nurse met them at the door. “This is a public hospital,” she said. “Many people visit here every day.”
Amal raised her hand. “Who works here?” she asked.
The nurse smiled. “Doctor Sarah works here. She helps people who are sick.” The class walked through the hall and passed clean, quiet rooms. Each room had beds, medicine and smiling workers.
“Exit the hospital quietly,” said Teacher Yasmin. “People are resting.”
Next, they walked to the court. “What happens here?” asked Nora.
“This is where problems are solved fairly,” said Teacher Yasmin. “An officer and a judge work here.” The building was large and painted white. A sign read: “Court — County of Arusha.”
Officer Rami met them inside. “Sit quietly on the wooden benches,” he said. “Can you see the big chair? That is where the judge sits.”
After the court, they walked to the market. Omar called out beside baskets of bananas, maize, and rice. “This place is busy,” said Amal. “Our village market is much smaller.”
“Some markets are small,” said Teacher Yasmin, “and others have a lot of things.” They bought sweet mangoes and sat near a small garden to eat.
“Next is the college,” said Teacher Yasmin.
“What is a college?” asked Sami.
“It is a school for older students,” said Teacher Yasmin. “There, they learn to be nurses, builders, or officers.”
At the college gate, Amal’s older brother Adam greeted them. “I study health and safety here,” he said.
“Do you want to be a doctor?” asked Nora.
“Yes,” said Adam, “because I want to help my village.”
As they walked back to the bus, Amal saw a sign by the road. “What is the border?” she asked.
“It is where one county ends and another begins,” said Teacher Yasmin. “That is the border between Arusha and Kilimanjaro counties.”
When they returned to school, Teacher Yasmin said, “Now write about what you saw.” Amal picked up her pencil and wrote:
“Today we went from our village to the county centre. We saw a hospital where Doctor Sarah works. We visited the court, the college, the market, and a garden. I learned that many people work to help us. I like the officer because he protects us. One day I want to be a sailor and see more places. But I will always come home.”
Teacher Yasmin smiled at her paper. “This is a good report,” she said. “It has details, and it shows what you learned.”
That evening, Amal shared her writing with Grandma Hana. “Very nice,” said Grandma Hana. “Your address may say ‘village,’ but your ideas travel far.”
Amal laughed. “Maybe one day I will see the sea and meet a sailor,” she said. “I might even cross a border.”
Grandma Hana nodded gently. “Yes. But never forget your village roots.”

comprehension.question: Name three places from “Our Community” and say what happens at each one.
comprehension.correctAnswer: The hospital is where a doctor helps sick people. The market is where people buy and sell food. The college is where older students learn new jobs.
comprehension.explanation: All three places sit in the middle of the text, each paired with the job it does for the community.

comprehension.question: Why does the community need older students to learn new jobs at the college?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because those students grow into the nurses, officers and builders the community needs.
comprehension.explanation: The learner has to join the college sentence to the helpers named earlier in the same passage.

comprehension.question: The poem calls the court grand. Which word does the poem give the market?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The poem gives the market the word busy, right beside grand for the court.
comprehension.explanation: Both describing words sit in the same line, so the learner reads the contrast off the poem.

comprehension.question: Why does the poem call the village slow but the market busy?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because only goats wander past the garden, while crowds fill the market.
comprehension.explanation: The learner has to weigh the goats against the crowds to explain the two describing words.

comprehension.question: In “At the Market”, what two foods does the speaker buy?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The speaker buys bananas and rice at the busy market.
comprehension.explanation: Both foods sit in one short sentence, so the learner scans the passage for the pair.

comprehension.question: Which three helpers does the song name, and what does each one do?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The doctor helps you when you are sick. The officer keeps you safe. The sailor sails across the sea.
comprehension.explanation: Each helper is paired with one action in the song, so all three pairs are needed for the mark.

comprehension.question: The song says the sailor sails across the sea. What does sails tell you?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It tells you the sailor travels on water in a boat or ship.
comprehension.explanation: The phrase across the sea gives the learner enough context to work out the verb.

comprehension.question: Where does Amal live, and is her village quiet or busy?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Amal lives in a small village near Mount Meru, and her village is quiet.
comprehension.explanation: The opening sentence of the story supplies both the place and the describing word.

comprehension.question: Which place did the class visit first, and who met them at the door?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They visited the hospital first, and a kind nurse met them at the door.
comprehension.explanation: The word first marks the order, so this checks that the learner tracked the sequence.

comprehension.question: Why did Teacher Yasmin say, “Exit the hospital quietly”?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because people were resting inside the hospital, and noise would disturb them.
comprehension.explanation: Teacher Yasmin gives her reason in the very next sentence, so the answer must quote it.

comprehension.question: Why does Adam want to become a doctor? Use because in your answer.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Adam wants to be a doctor because he wants to help his village.
comprehension.explanation: Adam states his own reason at the college gate, and the answer must keep the word because.

comprehension.question: What is a border? Which two counties meet at the border in this story?
comprehension.correctAnswer: A border is where one county ends and another begins. Arusha and Kilimanjaro counties meet there.
comprehension.explanation: Teacher Yasmin defines the word in the story, and the roadside sign names both counties.

grammar.title: Imperatives (Giving Instructions)
grammar.explanation: An imperative is a special way to use a verb to give a command or an instruction. It tells someone to do something. We need imperatives to give clear directions, like “Exit the court quietly” or “Go to the hospital if you are sick.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with the base verb (the action word), with no “to”: Go. / Stop. / Listen.
You do not say “you” — the person already knows you mean them: “Go to the market.”
You can add details to make it clear: “Exit the court quietly.”
To be polite, add please: “Please wash your hands.”
To say not to do something, use don't: “Don't run in the hall.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “You go to the hospital.” ✗
Fix: leave out “you” for a command: “Go to the hospital.” ✓
Mistake: “Quiet in the court.” ✗ Fix: do not forget the verb: “Be quiet in the court.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Imperatives are “action words for clear instructions.” If you can picture someone doing the action, you are using the imperative correctly!
grammar.practice: Choose the correct verb to complete each instruction: | ______ your hands before eating. (Wash / Washing) | ______ the door when you leave. (Close / Closes) | ______ to the teacher if you have a question. (Ask / Asking) | ______ your phone during class. (Use / Don't use)

grammar.title: Simple Present Tense
grammar.explanation: We use the simple present tense to talk about things that happen again and again, or facts that are always true. It helps us describe routines and how our community works, like “The market opens at 7 a.m.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use the base verb with: I, you, we, they.
They play football.
We visit the market.
I live in a village.
Add -s (or -es) with: he, she, it.
The doctor works at the hospital.
She visits her grandmother.
The market opens early.
If a verb ends in -y after a consonant, change y to i and add -es: study → studies.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “The doctor work at the hospital.” ✗
Fix: he, she and it need the extra “s”: “The doctor works at the hospital.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: This is your “everyday tense.” If you do it every day, use the simple present. And remember: “s” for he, she, it!
grammar.practice: Write the correct form of the verb in brackets: | My brother ______ (play) football after school. | She ______ (study) maths every day. | The sun ______ (rise) in the east. | They ______ (go) to the market on Fridays. | The nurse ______ (help) sick people.

grammar.title: Present Simple Questions
grammar.explanation: Good learners ask questions! Asking questions helps us find information and understand our community — like “Where is the college?” or “Do you know the address?”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use do with I, you, we, they: “Do you visit the market?”
Use does with he, she, it: “Does she work at the hospital?”
After do or does, use the base verb with no “s”: “Does he play football?”
You can start with a question word — what, where, when, who, how: “Where does the doctor work?”
With the verb to be, just move is or are to the front: “Is the hospital open?”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Does you like the market?” ✗ Fix: use do for “you”: “Do you like the market?” ✓
Mistake: “Where he is?” ✗ Fix: “Where is he?” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: “Do” is the friendly helper for many people (I, you, we, they). “Does” is for the one person who needs extra help (he, she, it).
grammar.practice: Complete each question with do or does and the base verb in brackets: | ______ you ______ (like) the market? | ______ he ______ (play) football? | ______ they ______ (visit) the college? | ______ she ______ (know) the address?

grammar.title: Using “can see” + object
grammar.explanation: We use “can see” to say what we are able to look at and notice around us. It makes our descriptions come alive: “I can see the border,” “She can see the garden from the market.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Subject + can + see + object (the thing you see).
I can see the market.
We can see the mountains.
She can see the garden from the window.
To ask: “Can you see the border?” To say no: “I cannot see the border.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She cans see the garden.” ✗ Fix: “can” never adds an “s”: “She can see the garden.” ✓
Mistake: “I can see.” with nothing after it ✗ Fix: add the object: “I can see the border.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of “can see” as your “magic eyes.” Always tell people what your magic eyes spot: subject + can + see + what you see.
grammar.practice: Complete each sentence with can see and finish the idea: | From the bus, I ______ the market. | They ______ the animals at the farm. | We ______ the garden from here. | She ______ the border on the map.

grammar.title: Using “because” for Explanations
grammar.explanation: The little word “because” is very powerful. It explains why something happens, or why we feel a certain way. For example: “I like the officer because he helps people.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Say your idea: “I like the officer.”
Add because: “I like the officer because …”
Give the reason: “… he helps people.”
Full sentence: “I like the officer because he helps people.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I like the market. It has fruit.” Two short sentences give the reader no reason word.
Fix: join them with because: “I like the market because it has fruit.” ✓
Also, “because” cannot stand alone: “Because it rained.” ✗ is not a full sentence. Say “We stayed home because it rained.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of “because” as a bridge. It connects your idea to the reason behind it. Every time you want to explain why, cross the “because” bridge.
grammar.practice: Finish each sentence with your own reason using “because”: | I like my teacher ______. | The market is my favourite place ______. | I would like to work at the hospital ______.

grammar.title: Descriptive Adjectives and Opposites + Quantifiers (some, a lot of)
grammar.explanation: Descriptive adjectives are words that describe nouns, such as people, places and things. Instead of “the village,” we can say “the quiet village.” Opposites help us compare two places clearly, and quantifiers tell us how much there is or how many there are.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Pick a noun to describe: “village.”
Choose an adjective: “quiet.”
Find its opposite: the opposite of “quiet” is “busy.”
Show the contrast in one sentence: “The village is quiet, but the county centre is busy.”
Useful opposites for this unit: quiet ↔ busy, small ↔ big, public ↔ private.
Use some for an amount that is not exact: “Some counties have many villages.”
Use a lot of for a large amount: “A lot of people visit the market.”
Both work with things you can count, such as villages, and things you cannot count, such as water.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “The village is more quiet than the town.” ✗
Fix: short words add -er: “The village is quieter than the town.” ✓
Mistake: “I do not have some friends.” ✗
Fix: in a negative sentence, use any: “I do not have any friends.” ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Picture a quiet village full of soft sounds beside a busy market full of people. Then picture some as a little sprinkle and a lot of as a big pile.
grammar.practice: Fill each blank with quiet or busy: | The garden is ______; the market is ______. | My room is ______; the county centre is ______. | Now fill each blank with some or a lot of: | There are ______ children playing in the garden. (a large crowd) | I need ______ help with my homework. (a little) | The market has ______ fruit today. (a huge amount) | We saw ______ birds near the border. (just a few)

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — My Community
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to say:
“In my community there is a ______.”
“At the ______, people ______.”
“I like the ______ because ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you name the place clearly? Did you give a reason with “because”? Did you speak loudly enough?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Listen to my community recording with my adult helper beside me, and tell me whether I named the place clearly and gave a real reason.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Giving Directions
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Use imperatives to give simple directions to a place. Say them out loud:
“Go straight to the market.”
“Turn left at the hospital.”
“Exit through the big door.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Check that every direction I say begins with a base verb, and tell me kindly when I slip the word “you” back in. An adult is sitting with me.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Guess the Place
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person thinks of a community place and gives a clue: “A doctor works here.”
The other person guesses: “Is it the hospital?”
Answer: “Yes, it is!” or “No, try again.” Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Play Guess the Place with me while my adult helper watches, and give me one extra clue only after I have made two guesses.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Sit with an adult and choose six words from your Word Wall. For each word, ask one question with do or does, then answer it in a full sentence. Try: “Does a sailor work at sea?” and “Yes, a sailor works on a ship at sea.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me one do or does question about each of my six chosen words, and tell me if my answers were full sentences. My adult helper is here.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Name your favourite place in the county centre and explain your choice with because. Then answer two follow-up questions from your partner without stopping to write anything down.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me two follow-up questions after I explain my favourite place, and check that my reason really follows the word because. An adult is listening.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan a one-minute tour of your community. Open with the place you like best, give three details about who works there, and close with one thing you can see from the road.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Time my one-minute community tour with my adult helper, then tell me which of my three details was the clearest and why.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Describe a Place
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Finish these sentences, then write two more of your own:
The ______ is a place where ______.
It is (quiet / busy).
I like it because ______.
At the ______ I can see ______.
writing.modelText: The hospital is a place where doctors and nurses care for people. It is busy. I like it because the nurses are kind. At the hospital I can see clean white beds and a long bright corridor.
writing.sentenceStarter: The hospital is a place where
writing.successCriteria: I named the place; I wrote whether it is quiet or busy; I gave a reason with because; I said what I can see there; I checked my capital letters.
writing.support: Point to the place on a picture and say each sentence out loud before you write it.
writing.extension: Add a second place and compare the two using but.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Your Own Poem
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write your own version of “Places I Know”. Change the places to ones near your home:
In my ______ the days are slow, / Past the ______ the ______ all go. / The ______ is busy, the ______ is grand, / So many places across our land!
Then draw a picture to go with your poem.
writing.modelText: In my village the days are slow, / Past the market the buses all go. / The clinic is busy, the college is grand, / So many places across our land!
writing.sentenceStarter: In my village the days are slow,
writing.successCriteria: I kept the four lines of the poem; I changed the places to my own; I made lines two and four rhyme; I read it aloud; I drew a matching picture.
writing.support: Brainstorm five places near your home and circle the two that rhyme.
writing.extension: Write a second verse about the people who work in those places.

writing.title: Writing 3 — A Report
writing.promptAndInstructions: Like Amal in the story, write a short report about a place you visited. Write three or four sentences with details. Begin: “Today I visited the ______.”
writing.modelText: Today I visited the county market. Omar sold bananas, maize and rice from big baskets. A lot of people pushed past us in the narrow lane. I liked the market because it smelled of ripe fruit.
writing.successCriteria: I opened with Today I visited; I gave three details; I used at least four unit words; I explained one reason; I finished with a closing sentence.
writing.support: List what you saw, who was there and how it felt before you begin writing.
writing.extension: Add a sentence about what you would like to see there next time.

writing.title: Writing 4 — Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write six sentences about the county centre. Begin with a topic sentence, then join your ideas with and, but and because.
writing.modelText: Our county centre is a busy place. It has a hospital and a court, and it also has a college. I like the garden best because it is quiet. Adam studies at the college, but he still comes home. The market opens early every morning. I can see the green hills from the college gate.
writing.successCriteria: I began with a topic sentence; I wrote six sentences; I joined ideas with and, but and because; I stayed on one subject; I reread my work aloud.
writing.support: Write your topic sentence first and read it back before adding the next idea.
writing.extension: Swap two short sentences for one longer sentence joined with because.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading called “Places in My County” and five true facts underneath. Use the simple present, such as “The market opens early.”
writing.modelText: Places in My County. The hospital cares for people who are sick. The court solves problems fairly. The college teaches older students new jobs. The market sells food every morning. The garden grows beans and tomatoes.
writing.successCriteria: I wrote a clear heading; I listed five true facts; I used the simple present; I used precise place words; I checked every full stop.
writing.support: Say each fact aloud and ask an adult whether it is really true.
writing.extension: Add a labelled diagram or table under your facts.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Read your report again. Rewrite three sentences with better describing words, add two new details, and explain one change you made.
writing.modelText: First I wrote that the market is nice. Now I write that the market is busy and full of colour. I added the smell of ripe mangoes and the sound of sellers calling. I changed the word nice because it did not help my reader picture the place.
writing.successCriteria: I chose an earlier piece; I rewrote three sentences; I added two new details; I explained one change; I proofread the whole piece.
writing.support: Underline the three weakest sentences before you rewrite anything.
writing.extension: Ask a partner to suggest one more improvement and try it.

activities.title: Activities 1 — Who Works Here? (Match)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each helper to the place where they work. Write the four pairs in your book.
doctor → ______
officer → ______
sailor → ______
college student → ______
Choose from these places: ship · hospital · college · road or court.
activities.answerSummary: Four matched pairs, with the officer allowed either road or court.

activities.title: Activities 2 — True or False (Community Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False for each sentence. If it is false, write the correct sentence underneath.
A doctor helps people who are sick.
A market is a place where people learn to swim.
A village is bigger than a county.
An exit is a way to go out of a place.
activities.answerSummary: Two true, two false, and each false statement rewritten correctly.

activities.title: Activities 3 — Fill in the Blanks
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose from: hospital · market · border · address · garden.
We buy fruit and vegetables at the ______.
Doctor Sarah works at the ______.
Flowers and tomatoes grow in the ______.
I wrote my home ______ on the letter.
The line where two counties meet is the ______.
activities.answerSummary: Five blanks filled, with every word from the box used exactly once.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Quiet or Busy?
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write “quiet” or “busy” for each place, then explain your choice with “because”. There is no single right answer. Example: “The village is quiet because there are few people.”
a village
a big market
a garden early in the morning
the county centre at noon
activities.answerSummary: Four labelled places, each followed by a reason joined with because.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort the thirteen Word Wall words into three sets: people who help us, places in town, and places on a map. Then explain the rule for each set in one complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Three sets holding three, six and four words, each with a stated sorting rule.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Hunt through “From Our Village to the County” for six details about places. Write each detail, then say what it teaches the reader about the county centre.
activities.answerSummary: Six different story details, each paired with what it shows about the county centre.

assignments.title: Places and Community learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Put together two pieces of work from this unit. First finish “Writing 3 — A Report” about a place you visited, using three details and one reason joined by because. Then record “Speaking 1 — My Community”, naming the same place and saying who works there. Marking guidance for the teacher: award up to 16 marks for the report against the writing rubric, looking for accurate place vocabulary, a clear order of ideas and correct capital letters and full stops; award up to 16 marks for the recording against the speaking rubric, looking for clear speech, at least four unit words and one complete because sentence. Read both pieces against the rubrics before you send them in together.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: Which word names the person who helps sick people feel better?
quizzes.explanation: Only the doctor cares for sick people; a sailor works at sea, a market sells food and a border is a line on a map.

quizzes.question: What does the word 'exit' mean?
quizzes.options: A line where two countries meet. | A school for older students and adults. | A person who works on a ship at sea. | A way to go out of a place.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A way to go out of a place.
quizzes.explanation: The other three options are the meanings of border, college and sailor.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Our Community': At the _____, people buy and sell food.
quizzes.explanation: The text pairs buying and selling with the market, not with a person, a home detail or a describing word.

quizzes.question: In the poem 'Places I Know', which place is called grand?
quizzes.options: the garden | the village | the court | the market
quizzes.explanation: The poem gives busy to the market and saves grand for the court in the very same line.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Community Helpers Song': The _____ keeps you safe.
quizzes.explanation: The song gives the sea to the sailor and the sick to the doctor, so only the officer keeps you safe.

quizzes.question: Which sentence gives an instruction?
quizzes.options: Exit the hall quietly. | The doctor works here. | Is the market busy? | We bought some mangoes.
quizzes.explanation: It opens with a base verb and leaves out the word you; the others are a statement, a question and a statement.

quizzes.question: Which question is written correctly?
quizzes.options: Does you like the market? | Do she know the address? | Where he is going today? | Do you like the market?
quizzes.explanation: You takes do, she takes does, and a question word must be followed by the verb: Where is he going today?

quizzes.question: Which sentence uses the simple present correctly?
quizzes.options: The market open at seven o'clock. | My brother go to college. | The doctor works at the hospital. | She visit her grandmother on Sunday.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The doctor works at the hospital.
quizzes.explanation: He, she and it add -s to the verb, so the other three sentences are all missing that ending.

quizzes.question: Which sentence gives a reason with because?
quizzes.options: I like the market because it has fresh fruit. | The quiet village sits between green farms and tall trees. | Exit the court quietly, please. | Because it rained.
quizzes.correctAnswer: I like the market because it has fresh fruit.
quizzes.explanation: Only this sentence joins a complete idea to its reason; the last option is not a full sentence at all.

quizzes.question: Which words show that a large number of people visit the market?
quizzes.options: some | a lot of | quiet | just a few
quizzes.explanation: A lot of means a large amount; some and just a few mean smaller amounts, and quiet describes a place.

liveSessions.title: Places and Community: Launch, vocabulary and learning goals
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read the unit overview and learn the three People Who Help Us words.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min word wall warm-up; 10 min Teacher Yasmin models naming places; 15 min paired place-and-helper matching; 10 min sentence building with can see; 5 min goal setting
liveSessions.afterSession: Add the six Places in Town words to your word wall and say each one aloud.

liveSessions.title: Places and Community: Reading fluency and comprehension
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read “Our Community” and the poem “Places I Know” aloud twice at home.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min fluency read of the poem; 10 min teacher think-aloud on grand and busy; 15 min guided comprehension of “Our Community”; 10 min partner question swap; 5 min review of new words
liveSessions.afterSession: Answer comprehension questions one to four in complete sentences.

liveSessions.title: Places and Community: Grammar workshop and feedback
liveSessions.beforeSession: Work through grammar lessons one to three on imperatives, simple present and questions.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min imperative chant; 10 min modelling the -s ending for he, she and it; 15 min do and does question practice; 10 min error hunt in mixed sentences; 5 min quick check
liveSessions.afterSession: Write four instructions for a visitor to your school, each starting with a base verb.

liveSessions.title: Places and Community: Dialogue, speaking and pronunciation
liveSessions.beforeSession: Record Speaking 1 and bring your Guess the Place clues to the session.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min tongue twisters with court and county; 10 min modelling a clear community description; 15 min Guess the Place in pairs; 10 min giving directions with imperatives; 5 min peer feedback
liveSessions.afterSession: Re-record Speaking 2 after listening to your teacher's feedback.

liveSessions.title: Places and Community: Writing conference and revision
liveSessions.beforeSession: Bring a first draft of Writing 3 to the session, with three details already chosen.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min sharing strong opening lines; 10 min modelling how to add details to a report; 15 min writing conference in threes; 10 min revising reasons with because; 5 min setting one improvement target
liveSessions.afterSession: Rewrite your report with two new details and a closing sentence.

liveSessions.title: Places and Community: Performance, assessment and reflection
liveSessions.beforeSession: Finish the poem in Writing 2 and practise reading it aloud with expression.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min poem performances; 10 min recap of quiet, busy, some and a lot of; 15 min unit quiz; 10 min self-assessment discussion; 5 min celebration and next-unit preview
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete the unit quiz and tick your self-assessment statements.

teacherNotes.note: Model every new place word aloud and check meaning before independent work, and let learners answer by speaking, drawing or writing. Keep an adult beside the child for every AI-tutor conversation and every recording task, and never leave a learner alone with the device. Give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read the poem with me and explain it?”
“Can you play a listening game about community helpers?”
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me practise talking about my community?”
“Can you play ‘Guess the Place’ with me?”
“Can you help me give clear directions to a place?”
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check if my sentences are correct?”
“Can you help me add more details to my report?”
“Can you help me make my poem rhyme?”
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you tell me more jobs that help our community?”
“What does a sailor do all day on a ship?”
“Can you quiz me on the people who help us?”
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me describe the market in my town?”
“What is the difference between a college and my school?”
“Can you quiz me on the places in town?”
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me learn my full address in English?”
“What is the difference between a village and a county?”
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on opposites like quiet and busy?”
“Can you help me use the word ‘public’ in a sentence?”
💬 Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Why do we say ‘the doctor works’ but ‘the doctors work’?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise imperatives?”
Every one of these prompts assumes an adult is sitting with the child.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: doctor → hospital; officer → road or court; sailor → ship; college student → college. Accept either road or court for the officer, because the story shows him working in both.
1 True. 2 False — a market is a place where people buy and sell food and other things. 3 False — a village is smaller than a county. 4 True.
1 market, 2 hospital, 3 garden, 4 address, 5 border. Every word from the box is used once, so a repeated word means one blank is still wrong.
There is no single right label. Award the mark when the child writes quiet or busy and follows it with a reason joined by because, such as “busy because many sellers shout”.
People who help us: doctor, officer, sailor. Places in town: hospital, court, college, market, garden, exit. Places on a map: village, county, border, address.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Six different story details, such as the white court building, the baskets of maize at the market, or Adam studying health and safety at the college gate.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: doctor. A sailor works at sea, a market is a place to buy food, and a border is a line on a map.
A way to go out of a place. The three wrong options define border, college and sailor instead.
market. The passage pairs buying and selling only with the market.
the court. The poem calls the market busy in the same line and saves grand for the court.
officer. The song hands the sea to the sailor and the sick to the doctor, leaving safety to the officer.
Exit the hall quietly. It begins with a base verb and drops the word you, which is what makes it an imperative.
Do you like the market? Use do with you, does with she, and put the verb straight after a question word.
The doctor works at the hospital. Watch for the missing -s in the three wrong sentences.
I like the market because it has fresh fruit. Point out that “Because it rained.” has no main idea in front of it.
a lot of. It signals a large amount, while some and just a few both signal a smaller one.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name places in my community and who works there.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can describe a place using words like quiet and busy.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can give an instruction using an imperative.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use the simple present (“the doctor works”).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can ask a question with “do” or “does”.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say what I can see, and explain a reason with “because”.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
